questions.text: I think that the most constructive thing I can do during someone's emotional crisis is to remain focused on identifying concrete solutions.
I feel satisfaction in maintaining streaks and unbroken chains of consistent effort.
I have difficulty sleeping after an emotionally intense experience because the feelings won't settle.
I tend to be unaware of the impression I am making on others in the moment.
I routinely choose the option that benefits others, even when a different choice would benefit me more.
I find it natural to give instructions and coordinate others.
I believe I have an obligation to contribute more than I consume.
I am sensitive to the tonal difference between genuine curiosity and interrogation.
I feel that my confidence in decisions helps reassure the people around me.
I hold the view that shortcuts usually cost more in the long run than doing things properly.
I feel that the moral claim of another's suffering is something I carry forward even when the moment of encounter has passed.
I experience the arrival of strong emotion as a wave that crests before I can brace for it.
I feel that the degree to which suffering functions as a genuine priority in my life is the best test of whether my moral convictions are real.
I believe that looking after my own needs is the foundation of a sustainable life.
I jump between tasks based on energy and interest rather than following a logical sequence.
My inner emotional life is relatively smooth and consistent.
I find that others naturally see me as a reliable collaborator.
I pick up on the subtle cues that indicate someone feels out of place in a group.
